content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Nancy A. Dillon, CMC 
City Clerk 
City of Thousand Oaks 
401 West Hillcrest Drive 
Thousand Oaks, CA 91360 
Dear Ms. Dillon: 
February 9, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-048 
You have written seeking information regarding the workshop 
and pending regulations concerning newsletters sent at public 
expense by or on behalf of incumbent candidates for election. 
This issue is governed by Government Code section 89001 which 
was recently amended to apply to local as well as state elected 
officers. 
I enclose copies of alternative versions of the regulation 
to implement Government Code section 89001. 
The Commission 
will consider adoption of one of these versions at its April 5, 
1988, meeting in Sacramento. 
Your comments are welcomed. 
In response to your question, this letter will confirm that 
the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter would be subject to the 
provisions of Government Code section 89001, if it is sent to 
200 or more households in a calendar month during the election 
cycle for the city and any incumbents are seeking re-election. 
The restrictions would also apply if one or more councilmembers 
were currently a candidate for any other elective office, 
local, state or federal. 
I trust that this letter adequately responds to your 
inquiry. 
Should you have furth~ questions, or comments on the 
proposed regulation, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901. 
REL: jaj 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
) 
By: 
Robert E. Leidigh 
Counsel, Legal Division 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

to the Commission. 
As stated in that letter, I 
would appreciate any additional information generated 
in that workshop. 
I would also like a response to 
the comments regarding the City of Thousand Oaks 
News 1 etter. 
Thank you. 
Sincerely,

;J~\ 
J.J 
NANCY A DILLON, CMC 
October 22, 1987 
California Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street, Suite 800 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804-0807 
Re: 
Mass Mailings at Public Expense after Public 
Official has Declared Candidacy 
G.C. Section 89001 
Workshop - October 29, 1987 
The City of Thousand Oaks in 1986 created an information newsletter 
to be sent to all residents. 
The first newsletter (Volume I, No. I - enclosed) was scheduled to 
be mailed during the summer of 1986. 
Due to the City Municipal 
election process which started in August with the candidate filings, 
the newsletter mailing was postponed until after the November 
election. 
All five Councilmembers were equally represented in the 
newsletter; however, two incumbents intended to file for re-election. 
The second newsletter (Volume II, No. I) was mailed Spring 1987 a 
non-election year. 
The third newsletter scheduled for late November is to be a tabloid 
and printed by the local newspaper. 
The City's goal is to release two newsletters per year. 
18901. 
Prohibition on Newsletter or Mass Mailing 
My interpretation of this section is that it would apply to the 
Thousand Oaks Newsletter during the election process for City Council 
candidates - even-numbered years August through November. 


California Fair Political Practices Commission 
October 22, 1987 
Page 2 
I would appreciate any additional information and confirmation re-
garding the Newsletter after your October 29th meeting. 
Sincerely, 
.

California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Nancy A. Dillon, CMC 
city Clerk 
P.o. Box 1496 
Thousand Oaks, CA 
91360 
Dear Ms. Dillon: 
January 27, 1988 
Re: 
88-048 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on January 27, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact Robert Leidigh, an attorney in 
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329) .) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
r)

Volume I, No. I 
City of Thousand Oaks 
Five facilities on drawing board 
Five major projects highlight the most 
significant Capital Improvements Program 
undertaken since City incorporation, 
according to Thousand Oaks Mayor Alex 
Fiore, outlining the planned $37.5 million 
investment. 
Citizens of all ages and with varied 
needs will be served by several new 
facilities, a Senior Center, Teen Center, 
Human Services Center, and the East Valley 
Sheriffs Station. Expansion of the Thousand 
Oaks Civic Center will provide needed 
handicapped access. 
"The facilities are in various stages of 
development It's an ambitious program 
made possible because the City planned and 
saved ... ," said Fiore, who has served on 
the City Council since incorporation. He 
explained fmancial strategies undertaken to 
reach the goal; the tools of prudent cash 
management, systematic annual 
appropriation and saving of funds for 
designated projects have been utilized. 
Approximately $10 million has been 
targeted for general improvements, $1.4 
(See Projects. Page 3) 
Proposed Civic Cenier Addition 
Expansion program commences 
Plans include handicapped access 
A two-story addition to the City of 
Thousand Oaks Civic Center, part of the 
remodeling and expansion currently in 
progress will add needed space and 
refinements to the complex dedicated 13 
years ago. 
"The new facility will include a City 
Council chamber, enhanced handicapped 
access and a main entryway complete with a 
receptlOnlmlronnatlon center," said City 
Councilman Lawrence E. Homer. 
The initial facility was built to take 
care of the City's needs through 1985. Since 
its dedication, Feb. 24, 1973, the 
community's population has nearly doubled, 
going from approximately 50,000 to 
97,000 residents. 
"It was becoming necessary to rent 
office space that would meet City needs or 
construct an additional building," said Public 
Works Department Director Lou Scherer, in 
charge of the facility expansion project 
"That happens in the private sector 
also; recently our community newspaper 
expanded their offices, they simply outgrew 
the old building," Homer noted. 
An additional 34,000 square feet will be 
added when the two-story structure between 
the existing adjacent buildings is completed. 
(See Civic Center. Page 4) 
Teens and seniors may soon find the 
empty acres next to the City of Thousand 
Oaks Library a thriving nucleus of activities. 
Within 12 to 18 months two separate 
centers, one for teens and one for seniors, 
could be open and operating at the Conejo 
Creek park site, says Councilman Lee 
Laxdal. 
Facilities to serve both segments of the 
community have been long-standing 
priorities which are now being realized. 
"Teens and seniors have specialized needs 
and we're working to meet them," says 
Mayor Pro Tern Frank Schillo, describing 
extensive efforts involving separate 
committees composed of City Council-
members, Conejo Recreation and Park 
District representatives, and citizens. 
Schillo also serves on the Senior/Adult 
Center Committee. Laxdal chairs the 
Senior/Adult Center Committee and is a 
Teen Center Committee member. 
(See Teen. Senior Centers. Page 3) 
Citizens' Traffic 
Committee task: 
lending assistance 
New structures are being built, but 
there's more to Thousand Oaks' capital 
improvements than concrete and steel centers 
serving local citizens. The community's 
labyrinth of well-maintained streets, neatly 
manicured landscaping and other 
infrastructure elements are essential 
segments of a quality community, says City 
Councilwoman Madge Schaefer. 
As chair of the IS-member Citizen 
Traffic Committee, which identifies, 
discusses and seeks resolution to traffic 
concerns, Schaefer is well aware of the 
importance of street overlay programs, traffic 
signals, adequate water and wastewater 
facilities to a community. 
(See Traffic, Page 4) 


Informed individuals, willing to serve ... 
Committees, Commissions and 
Boards: input forms vital link 
Open space preservation, noise 
abatement, library fmancing, and 
housing for low-and moderate-income 
families are among the myriad of issues 
that have been addressed by Thousand 
Oaks citizens serving on City 
Committees, Commissions or Boards. 
"The legacy of elected leaders 
working with Thousand Oaks residents is 
strong. The City's General Plan was 
devised by a lOO-member citizen 
committee," said Mayor Frank Schillo. 
"Citizen-government interaction has 
molded our City into its present form." 
"The enthusiasm generated during 
incorporation continued ... ," says 
Councilman Alex Fiore, a Council 
member since 1964. 
Initially, City Council set goals 
and the staff worke< to reach those 
objectives. After establishment of the 
(See Citizens. Page 3) 
'It's a great opportunity •.. The possibilities 
are unlimited because youth have direct 
input into City government.' 
Commissioner Daric Kozlowski 
V olunteers are 
great resource 
Persons serving on a City of 
Thousand Oaks Committee, 
Commission or Board may handle a 
variety of tasks. One thing is certain, in 
the opinion of Mayor Pro Tern Lee 
Laxdal, their volunteer work is 
"invaluable, the community's greatest 
resource." 
Councilman Larry Homer says, 
"Citizens' advisory committees are very 
important ... not just at City Hall but in 
every governmental agency." 
"We all benefit from the efforts of 
people who work on solving problems, 
whether in support of or opposition to 
an issue. They grind out solutions, set 
goals, get things done," Laxdal said. 
Citizens serve in an advisory 
capacity to the City Council. "This 
fosters greater community participation 
in local government and provides elected 
leaders assistance in setting goals," 
Homer noted. 
Commissions and Boards are created 
either by the City Council adopting a 
resolution (POlicy) or ordinance (law), 
while Committees are generally formed 
by Council action. Some Commissions, 
like Planning, are considered permanent. 
Other groups are created to assist with 
specific tasks, such as developing 
recommendations for the Teen Center or 
Senior Adult Center. Once the job is

sections.question: You have written seeking information regarding the workshop and pending regulations concerning newsletters sent at public expense by or on behalf of incumbent candidates for election. Specifically, would the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter be subject to the provisions of Government Code section 89001 during the election cycle when incumbents are seeking re-election?
sections.conclusion: In response to your question, this letter will confirm that the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter would be subject to the provisions of Government Code section 89001, if it is sent to 200 or more households in a calendar month during the election cycle for the city and any incumbents are seeking re-election. The restrictions would also apply if one or more councilmembers were currently a candidate for any other elective office, local, state or federal.
sections.question_synthetic: The City of Thousand Oaks sought clarification on whether its citywide newsletter sent at public expense would be subject to Government Code section 89001 restrictions during election cycles when city council incumbents are candidates for re-election.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirmed that the City of Thousand Oaks newsletter is subject to Government Code section 89001 restrictions if sent to 200+ households monthly during election cycles when incumbents are seeking re-election, including candidacy for other offices.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document file contains multiple letters in sequence: incoming request (Oct 22, 1987), acknowledgment letter (Jan 27, 1988), follow-up from requester (Jan 20, 1988), and formal FPPC advice response (Feb 9, 1988). Primary response letter is dated February 9, 1988. Question and conclusion sections synthesized from content rather than formal section headers.

QUESTION: You have written seeking information regarding the workshop and pending regulations concerning newsletters sent at public expense by or on behalf of incumbent candidates for election. Specifically, would the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter be subject to the provisions of Government Code section 89001 during the election cycle when incumbents are seeking re-election?

CONCLUSION: In response to your question, this letter will confirm that the City of Thousand Oaks' newsletter would be subject to the provisions of Government Code section 89001, if it is sent to 200 or more households in a calendar month during the election cycle for the city and any incumbents are seeking re-election. The restrictions would also apply if one or more councilmembers were currently a candidate for any other elective office, local, state or federal.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the City of Thousand Oaks regarding whether its resident newsletter is subject to Government Code section 89001 restrictions on mass mailings at public expense during election cycles. The FPPC confirms the newsletter is subject to these restrictions when sent to 200+ households during election cycles with incumbent candidates seeking re-election.
